pathophysiology_summary: Reactivation of latent varicella-zoster virus in a single dorsal root ganglion produces the pathognomonic triad: severe preceding neuralgic pain/hyperaesthesia, a strictly unilateral dermatomal distribution stopping at the midline (here T5–T6), and grouped vesicles on an erythematous base, with regional lymphadenopathy and mild systemic upset. The classic red herring is that dermatomal thoracic pain precedes the rash and can mimic acute coronary syndrome or PE, especially in an older adult — a remote provider cannot auscultate or perform an ECG, so although the dermatomal rash strongly confirms zoster, a cardiac/respiratory screen and a residual safety-net are retained. Management priorities are prompt oral antivirals within ~72 hours of rash onset (to speed healing and reduce postherpetic neuralgia) and neuropathic analgesia (e.g., pregabalin/amitriptyline) as simple analgesia is often insufficient. Must-not-miss considerations also include ophthalmic zoster if V1 is involved (Hutchinson sign — negative here) and disseminated zoster (multi-dermatomal/widespread) suggesting immunosuppression. Advise keeping lesions dry/uncovered and avoiding pregnant women and non-immune infants until crusted.

differential.evidence_basis: Severe unilateral dermatomal neuralgic pain then grouped vesicles stopping at the midline.
Severe chest pain in an older adult — screen for cardiac/respiratory features; cannot ECG remotely.
Itchy, not deeply neuropathic, and not strictly dermatomal.
A hot spreading plaque, not grouped clear vesicles with neuropathic pain.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Confirm dermatomal zoster and start antivirals promptly, but screen for cardiac/respiratory causes given the chest pain and age; use neuropathic analgesia.

differential.evidence_basis: Dermatomal vesicular rash with neuropathic pain; no exertional/crushing pain, radiation, sweating or true breathlessness.
Lower probability given the dermatomal rash and non-cardiac features, but cannot be fully excluded remotely — retain a safety-net.
Working diagnosis: prompt antivirals within the window, neuropathic analgesia, lesion care, and a cardiac/respiratory and dissemination safety-net.

red_flags.description: Cardiac/respiratory red flags: crushing/exertional chest pain, radiation to arm/jaw, sweating, or true breathlessness
red_flags.clinical_significance: ACS/PE cannot be excluded remotely (no ECG/auscultation); these features would mandate emergency cardiac assessment.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A concurrent cardiac event could be missed.

red_flags.description: Disseminated zoster (lesions crossing the midline / multiple dermatomes / widespread) or ophthalmic involvement
red_flags.clinical_significance: Suggests immunosuppression or sight-threatening ophthalmic zoster needing urgent specialist care.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missed disseminated/ophthalmic zoster can be serious.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Anchoring on zoster and missing ACS
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Attributing all chest pain to zoster without screening for cardiac/respiratory features (which cannot be examined remotely).
Relying on simple analgesia alone for severe zoster neuralgia.
Delaying antivirals beyond the ~72h window, increasing postherpetic neuralgia risk.

telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Start oral antivirals promptly (within the window) and neuropathic analgesia; screen for cardiac/respiratory features and retain a safety-net; ED/000 if any cardiac or respiratory red flags or dissemination.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Attributing chest pain solely to zoster and missing a concurrent cardiac event, or under-treating the neuralgia.

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Start antivirals for herpes zoster within 72 hours of rash onset (especially in older adults) to reduce severity and postherpetic neuralgia; provide neuropathic analgesia; refer ophthalmic/disseminated zoster.
